serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tau sar piah piece weighs approximately 50 g, which is a typical single-serve portion sold at traditional Singaporean bakeries and confectionery shops.

nutrient_source.title: Tau Sar Piah (Red Bean) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tau Sar Piah (Red Bean), CR ID F11110026. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary regional source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tau sar piah in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous sweet filled pastry / shortcrust pastry with sweet bean filling. The high refined flour and sugar content (51.6g carbohydrate, 21.4g sugar per 100g) supports a medium-to-high GI estimate consistent with similar baked sweet pastries in the Sydney GI database. Glycemic load per 50g serving = (65 × 25.8g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 16.8g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (21.4g/100g) with borderline fibre (2.35g/100g) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention; sodium itself is low at 37mg/100g but the high fat and sugar load contributes to metabolic blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tau sar piah to occasional small portions, as the high sugar and saturated fat content (~10g/100g) can elevate blood pressure indirectly through insulin resistance and arterial stiffness, despite the relatively low direct sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 21.4g/100g exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling; total carbohydrate 51.63g/100g with only 2.35g dietary fibre results in rapid glucose absorption and a medium-to-high glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit tau sar piah due to its high sugar content (21.4g/100g) and high refined carbohydrate load (51.63g/100g), which will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, restrict to half a piece and pair with a protein source to partially attenuate the glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 10g/100g and estimated trans fat 0.2g/100g from shortening, combined with high refined sugar 21.4g/100g, present a combined atherogenic and pro-inflammatory risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid tau sar piah regularly, as the combination of high saturated fat (~10g/100g), estimated trans fat from hydrogenated shortening (~0.2g/100g), and high sugar (21.4g/100g) raises LDL cholesterol, promotes systemic inflammation, and increases cardiovascular event risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat 0.2g/100g from shortening, saturated fat ~10g/100g, and refined sugar 21.4g/100g are all pro-inflammatory factors that worsen RA joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid tau sar piah made with lard or hydrogenated shortening, as the combined pro-inflammatory fat and sugar profile outweighs the modest fibre and polyphenol benefit from the red bean filling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tau Sar Piah (Red Bean), known in Chinese as 豆沙饼（红豆）, is a traditional Teochew-origin flaky pastry widely sold in Singapore at heritage bakeries and confectionery shops. The pastry consists of a thin, multi-layered flaky shell made from wheat flour and lard or vegetable shortening, encasing a generous filling of sweetened red bean (adzuki bean) paste. The texture is characteristically crumbly and flaky on the outside with a dense, moist, and sweet interior. The flavour profile is mildly sweet with an earthy red bean note. Per 100g, tau sar piah provides 454 kcal, 6.75g protein, 24.55g fat (of which approximately 10g is saturated fat), 51.63g carbohydrate, 21.4g sugar, 2.35g dietary fibre, and 37mg sodium. The high fat content derives from the layered shortening-based pastry shell, while the high sugar content comes from the sweetened red bean filling. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sugar (21.4g/100g) and refined carbohydrate load. It poses moderate-to-high cardiovascular risk due to saturated and trans fat from shortening. Sodium is low, but the overall metabolic profile warrants caution for hypertensive individuals. Tau sar piah is a culturally significant Singaporean snack often consumed during festive occasions, as a teatime snack, or as a gift item. It is generally considered vegetarian but not vegan if lard is used, and is not halal-certified when lard-based.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tau sar piah red bean?
Is tau sar piah suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of tau sar piah?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11110026), which is the approved primary regional source for Singaporean foods. Exact match confirmed. Saturated fat estimated at ~10g/100g and trans fat at ~0.2g/100g based on typical shortening-based pastry composition as HPB does not report these sub-fractions for this entry; these are documented as estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework as no direct measurement exists for this specific pastry.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid sub-fraction data for tau sar piah not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11110026. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this entry as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g set to 0.2 as an estimate based on typical hydrogenated shortening content in traditional Teochew flaky pastry; HPB does not report trans fat for this entry. saturated_fat_g set to 10.2 as an estimate based on typical lard/shortening-based pastry composition; HPB does not report saturated fat sub-fractions for this entry. Halal status is uncertain as traditional versions may use lard; consumers should verify with the specific bakery. Vegan status is false as lard is commonly used in traditional preparations. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tau sar piah exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
